summary: Well-scoped, additive plan that completes the three-lever quality system (pixel ratio, point count, shader complexity) without altering existing architectural patterns. The current codebase already handles canvas sizing correctly, so this is purely about extending the quality profile to control GPU shader cost.

interface_changes.note: All downstream consumers (main.ts, tests) must be updated, but the change is mechanical — no logic rethinking required
New fields can default to current behavior (3 octaves, both enabled) for backward compat

architectural_impact.data_flow_changes: Extends existing quality→main→systems→shaders pipeline with 3 new uniform channels. No new data flow paths created.

files_at_risk.reason: layeredNoise refactor to conditional octaves changes the core deformation function. Must ensure visual continuity at high tier (3 octaves) matches current output exactly.
Same layeredNoise refactor. Both shaders must be updated in lockstep.
Mechanical updates to assertions for new QualityProfile fields. No test logic changes.

notes: Plan correctly identifies that canvas CSS sizing is already correct — no layout bug to fix
Plan correctly identifies that enableSparkle only gates a single if-branch (line 120-122 in pointWarp.vert.glsl: trebleEnergy > 0.5 sparkle), confirming it's not a meaningful GPU cost lever
Plan correctly identifies the 3-octave layeredNoise as the primary ALU cost center (lines 29-34 in both shaders)
Plan correctly notes shaders have no quality awareness — all 18 uniforms are set identically regardless of tier

risks.description: GLSL integer uniform branching for octave count
risks.detail: Using `uniform int uNoiseOctaves` with if-guards may not yield GPU savings on all hardware due to SIMD execution. However, the plan acknowledges this and correctly notes that the primary savings come from fewer sin() evaluations, not branch prediction.
risks.mitigation: Plan's approach is acceptable. Could alternatively use #define with shader variants, but that adds complexity disproportionate to the benefit.

risks.description: layeredNoise normalization changes across octave counts
risks.detail: Current layeredNoise divides by 1.75 (sum of weights 1.0+0.5+0.25). With 1 octave, dividing by 1.75 would dampen the output. With 2 octaves (weight sum 1.5), same issue. The plan doesn't mention adjusting the normalization divisor per octave count.
risks.mitigation: Normalize per active octave count: 1 octave → /1.0, 2 octaves → /1.5, 3 octaves → /1.75. This preserves visual amplitude across tiers.

risks.detail: Plan places context loss handling in scene.ts. The renderer state restoration on contextrestored needs to also re-upload all BufferGeometry and ShaderMaterial uniforms. Three.js handles most of this automatically, but custom attributes (aHueOffset, aRandom) may need needsUpdate flags.
risks.mitigation: After contextrestored, set needsUpdate=true on all BufferAttribute instances, or simply re-initialize geometry systems.

suggestions.description: Adjust layeredNoise normalization divisor based on octave count to preserve visual amplitude consistency across tiers
Consider also gating the bass directional drift block (lines 76-79 in pointWarp) on low tier — it's 3 additional sin/cos calls per vertex that could be skipped
In the context loss handler (step 5), after contextrestored, trigger needsUpdate on all custom BufferAttributes (aHueOffset, aRandom) to ensure Three.js re-uploads them to the new GL context

gaps: No test for layeredNoise normalization consistency across octave counts (visual regression risk R2)
No test for context loss/restore cycle — hard to unit test but could verify event listener registration
